title: Switch Statements
description: Select one of many code blocks to be executed.

slides.title: The switch Statement 🔀
slides.content:
Instead of writing many `else if` statements to check the same variable for equality against different values, use `switch`.

The `switch` expression is evaluated once, and its value is compared with the values of each `case`.

slides.title: Example
slides.content: Checking the value of the 'day' variable.

quizData.question: What will be printed by the switch statement above?
quizData.explanation: Since variable 'day' equals 3, the compiler executes code inside 'case 3:'.

slides.title: break & default 🛑
slides.content:
When the compiler reaches a `break` keyword, it immediately breaks out of the switch block.

The `default` keyword specifies some code to run if there is no case match (like an `else` at the end of `else if`).

quizData.question: What happens if you completely forget to write the 'break;' keyword at the end of a matched case?

options.text: It executes that case AND "falls through" to execute the code in the next following cases too

quizData.explanation: If there's no break, C++ will continue to execute the code in the subsequent cases, regardless of whether they match the condition. This is called 'fall-through'.
